trust_assumption: A domain controller trusts any principal holding the directory-replication rights (DS-Replication-Get-Changes/-All) to request account secrets over MS-DRSR as if it were a peer DC.
violation: An attacker who has been granted or has stolen replication rights wields them to pull password hashes for any account, including krbtgt, without ever logging into a DC or touching its disk.

techniques.name: DCSync replication of credentials (Mimikatz lsadump::dcsync, secretsdump)

trust_assumption: The directory trusts inbound replication from any object registered as a domain controller in the configuration partition, accepting its pushed changes as authoritative origin data.
violation: An attacker temporarily registers a rogue DC and pushes attribute changes (SIDHistory, group membership) via replication, laundering a malicious edit as legitimate DC-to-DC provenance that bypasses change logging.

trust_assumption: The NTDS.dit database file is trusted to be accessible only to the DC's directory service, while the OS exposes a consistent copy of it through volume shadow copies and backup APIs to any local administrator.
violation: A local admin on the DC snapshots the volume or invokes ntdsutil/VSS to read NTDS.dit and the SYSTEM hive offline, extracting the entire domain's hashes from a file the auth subsystem assumed only it could open.

techniques.name: NTDS.dit extraction via Volume Shadow Copy / ntdsutil (secretsdump -ntds)

trust_assumption: The authentication endpoint trusts that repeated login attempts spread thinly across many accounts and slowly over time are legitimate traffic that stays under its per-account lockout and rate thresholds.
violation: An attacker exploits the gap between per-account lockout and aggregate volume, trying a few common passwords against the whole user population over days until weak credentials are guessed without tripping lockout.

techniques.name: Password spraying and credential brute force across the auth surface

trust_assumption: The user and the IdP both trust the login flow they see, assuming the page relaying the real authentication and the post-MFA session cookie it mints belong to the legitimate endpoints.
violation: A reverse-proxy phishing portal relays the genuine login and MFA challenge to the real IdP, then captures the issued session cookie, defeating MFA by stealing the already-authenticated context rather than the password.

techniques.name: AiTM reverse-proxy phishing and session-cookie capture (Evilginx, Modlishka)

trust_assumption: A web application trusts that a presented session cookie or saved credential is bound to the user it was issued to, accepting it as proof of an authenticated session regardless of which device replays it.
violation: An infostealer reads cookies, saved passwords, and tokens out of the browser's local profile and exfiltrates them, letting the operator import a live session on another machine with no login and no MFA.

techniques.name: Infostealer browser cookie/credential theft and session import (RedLine, Lumma, Raccoon)

trust_assumption: The OS input stack trusts any in-session process registering a keyboard hook or reading the input device as a legitimate participant entitled to observe keystrokes destined for other windows.
violation: Attacker code installs a hook or hooks the input API to record everything the user types, capturing passwords, MFA codes, and passphrases as they are entered into other applications.

techniques.name: Keylogging via input hooks / keyboard API

trust_assumption: Cloud APIs trust the bearer of a cached CLI or browser access/refresh token, which the SDK stores on local disk in the clear so a tool can reuse the session without re-prompting.
violation: An attacker with local access reads the cached token files (.aws/credentials, azureProfile/msal cache, gcloud, kube config) and replays them from anywhere to act as the user against the cloud control plane.

trust_assumption: The Terminal Services session manager trusts a console operator or SYSTEM-level caller to reconnect to a disconnected RDP session as that session's owner, without re-presenting the owner's credentials.
violation: An attacker running as SYSTEM uses tscon to attach to another user's disconnected session, inheriting their authenticated desktop, mapped resources, and cached credentials with no password prompt.

trust_assumption: A jump host is trusted to forward the user's SSH agent socket and accept inherited authorized_keys/known_hosts, treating any process that can reach the agent socket as the key owner authorizing onward connections.
violation: An attacker on the jump host reaches the forwarded agent socket (or hijacks a multiplexed control socket) and signs authentications to downstream hosts as the user, pivoting along trust chains without holding the private key.

techniques.name: SSH agent-forwarding hijack and key-trust pivoting

trust_assumption: DCOM trusts a remote object activation as the legitimate intent of the authenticated calling principal, executing the invoked method (MMC20, ShellWindows, Excel/Outlook automation) under that identity on the target.
violation: An attacker with valid credentials remotely activates an exposed DCOM object and drives its exposed methods to run commands on the target, achieving lateral execution through a sanctioned RPC interface rather than a remote-service tool.

techniques.name: DCOM lateral execution (MMC20.Application, ShellWindows, Office COM)

trust_assumption: Each STS role-assumption hop trusts its own trust policy in isolation, validating only the immediate caller's right to assume the next role without reasoning about the full chain of identities traversed to reach it.
violation: An attacker walks a chain of permissive role-trust and PassRole relationships, assuming role after role across accounts to reach a privileged identity no single policy intended the starting principal to wield.

techniques.name: Cloud assume-role chaining and PassRole pivoting

trust_assumption: The egress and segmentation controls trust protocol-conformant outbound traffic (HTTPS/WebSocket/DNS) as ordinary application flows, inspecting the carrier protocol rather than the arbitrary sessions framed inside it.
violation: An attacker tunnels a SOCKS proxy or reverse pivot inside an allowed protocol, framing internal-network connections as benign egress so the operator reaches segmented hosts through a channel the firewall treats as conformant.

techniques.name: SOCKS proxy / protocol tunneling pivot (Chisel, Ligolo-ng, reverse SOCKS)
